title: Topic 416: The Psychology of Virtual Tourism: Travel Through Gaming
content:
### Abstract

This paper embarks on a whimsical yet academically rigorous journey into the burgeoning phenomenon of virtual tourism, where intrepid explorers trade passports for pixels and embark on epic quests from the comfort of their ergonomic gaming chairs. We investigate the psychological underpinnings of this digital wanderlust, exploring how meticulously crafted virtual worlds can evoke a genuine sense of place, inspire real-world travel, and, most importantly, provide a valid excuse for spending eight consecutive hours in front of a screen. Through a critical analysis of existing literature, a dash of armchair psychology, and a generous sprinkling of real-game examples, we reveal the synergistic relationship between gaming and tourism. We argue that video games are not merely a form of escapism but a powerful new medium for cultural dissemination and a surprisingly effective, if unconventional, form of destination marketing. This paper will be of interest to academics, marketing professionals, and anyone who has ever justified a gaming marathon as a 'cultural experience.'

### Introduction

Once upon a time, in an era now confined to the sepia-toned annals of history (or at least, pre-2000s photo albums), the concept of 'travel' involved a rather cumbersome process. It necessitated packing, queuing, enduring the indignities of airport security, and the very real possibility of sitting next to a screaming baby for six hours. Fast forward to the 21st century, and a new breed of tourist has emerged: the virtual tourist. Armed with a high-spec PC, a comfortable headset, and a beverage of their choice, these digital adventurers are exploring the majestic landscapes of Hyrule, the bustling streets of Night City, and the historically accurate, albeit slightly stabby, cities of Renaissance Italy. This paper delves into the fascinating psychology of this phenomenon, asking the crucial question: is virtual tourism a legitimate form of travel, or just a very elaborate form of procrastination? We will explore how video games, once dismissed as a frivolous pastime for basement-dwelling teenagers, have evolved into sophisticated cultural artifacts capable of inspiring genuine emotional connections to virtual places. We will examine the psychological mechanisms at play, from the 'narrative transportation theory' that explains our deep immersion in game worlds to the 'place attachment' that makes us feel a genuine sense of belonging to a digital space. Furthermore, we will investigate the surprising spillover of this virtual tourism into the real world, where gamers are increasingly inspired to visit the real-life locations that feature in their favorite games. So, grab your virtual passport, suspend your disbelief, and join us on a journey into the heart of the digital frontier. Just be sure to save your game first.

### Literature Review

The hallowed halls of academia have, in their infinite wisdom, begun to turn their scholarly gaze towards the curious intersection of video games and tourism. A growing body of research suggests that this is not merely a niche interest but a significant cultural and economic phenomenon. For instance, a study by Nikolić (2025) posits that video games “allow the presentation and promotion of tourist destinations and their attractions realistically and in detail through the game world, while players are motivated to visit these destinations in the real world.” This suggests that the meticulously crafted environments of modern video games can serve as a surprisingly effective form of advertising, a kind of interactive travel brochure that you can explore at your own pace. The study further highlights the potential for a “synergistic framework” between the gaming and tourism industries, a concept that has been echoed by other researchers. For example, a 2025 study on the impact of the game *Black Myth: Wukong* on tourism in Shanxi province, China, found that the game's release led to a 156% surge in tourism to the region. The authors of this study propose a “cognitive-affective-conative (CAC) framework” to explain this phenomenon, suggesting that the cognitive experience of the game (learning about the culture and history of the region) leads to an affective response (a feeling of connection and nostalgia), which in turn leads to a conative action (booking a flight to Shanxi). This framework provides a useful lens through which to understand the psychological journey from player to tourist. However, the literature is not without its gaps. Much of the existing research focuses on the positive aspects of video game tourism, with less attention paid to the potential downsides. For example, what happens when the real-world location doesn't live up to its digital counterpart? Is there a risk of 'virtual disappointment syndrome'? And what about the ethical implications of using video games to promote tourism to sensitive or over-touristed areas? These are questions that future research would do well to explore. In the meantime, the current literature provides a solid foundation for understanding the burgeoning field of video game tourism, a field that is likely to become increasingly important as the lines between the virtual and the real continue to blur.

### Analysis

The psychological appeal of virtual tourism is a multifaceted phenomenon, a delicious cocktail of escapism, exploration, and wish fulfillment. At its core, it taps into the same fundamental human desires that have driven explorers and adventurers for centuries: the desire to see new places, to experience different cultures, and to boldly go where no one has gone before (or at least, where no one has gone before without a decent Wi-Fi connection). One of the key psychological mechanisms at play is the concept of 'presence,' the feeling of 'being there' in a virtual environment. This is achieved through a combination of high-fidelity graphics, immersive sound design, and interactive gameplay. When a game successfully creates a sense of presence, the player is no longer just controlling a character on a screen; they are *in* the game world, experiencing it with their own senses. This can lead to a powerful emotional connection to the virtual environment, a phenomenon known as 'place attachment.' Just as we can become attached to our real-world homes and neighborhoods, we can also become attached to our virtual homes and neighborhoods. This is why so many players of *Animal Crossing: New Horizons* felt a genuine sense of loss when their virtual islands were deleted. Another important psychological factor is the 'narrative transportation theory,' which suggests that we can become so absorbed in a story that we are 'transported' into the world of the narrative. Video games, with their interactive narratives and player-driven stories, are particularly effective at inducing this state of transportation. When we play a game like *The Witcher 3: Wild Hunt*, we are not just following the story of Geralt of Rivia; we *are* Geralt of Rivia, making choices that shape the world around us. This deep level of immersion can lead to a profound appreciation for the game's world, and a desire to experience it in a more tangible way. This is where the link to real-world tourism comes in. When a game is set in a real-world location, the player's virtual experience can create a powerful desire to visit that location in person. This is not just about seeing the sights; it's about walking in the footsteps of their virtual avatar, of experiencing the real-world version of the place they have come to know and love in the digital realm. It is a pilgrimage of sorts, a journey to the holy land of their favorite game.

### Real-World Examples

The phenomenon of video game-inspired tourism is not just a theoretical concept; it is a real and growing trend. There are countless examples of gamers flocking to real-world locations that have been featured in their favorite games. One of the most famous examples is the town of Český Krumlov in the Czech Republic, which is said to have inspired the setting of the game *Kingdom Come: Deliverance*. The game, which is set in 15th-century Bohemia, is renowned for its historical accuracy and its faithful recreation of the region's architecture and landscape. As a result, the town has seen a significant increase in tourism from gamers who want to experience the real-life version of the game's world. Another example is the island of Tsushima in Japan, the setting for the game *Ghost of Tsushima*. The game, which tells the story of a samurai warrior fighting against the Mongol invasion of Japan, has been praised for its stunning depiction of the island's natural beauty. In the wake of the game's release, the island has seen a surge in tourism, with many visitors citing the game as their reason for visiting. The local tourism association has even embraced the trend, offering 'Ghost of Tsushima' themed tours of the island. But it's not just historical and fantasy games that are inspiring tourism. The gritty, futuristic world of *Cyberpunk 2077* has inspired some fans to visit the real-life locations in California that inspired the game's setting of Night City. And the post-apocalyptic landscapes of the *Fallout* series have led some intrepid gamers to visit the real-life ruins of Chernobyl. These examples demonstrate the power of video games to create a new form of tourism, one that is driven by a desire to connect with the virtual worlds that have captured our imaginations.

### Discussion

The rise of virtual tourism has significant implications for our understanding of human behavior and consumer habits. It challenges our traditional notions of what it means to 'travel' and to 'experience' a place. It suggests that the boundaries between the real and the virtual are becoming increasingly blurred, and that our digital experiences can have a profound impact on our real-world lives. From a consumer behavior perspective, virtual tourism represents a new and powerful form of marketing. It is a form of 'experiential marketing' that allows consumers to 'try before they buy,' to experience a destination in a virtual setting before committing to a real-world trip. This has the potential to be far more effective than traditional forms of tourism marketing, such as brochures and television commercials. It also suggests that the gaming industry has the potential to become a major player in the tourism market. Game developers are, in a sense, the new travel agents, creating virtual worlds that inspire us to explore the real world. This opens up a whole new world of possibilities for cross-industry collaboration, with tourism boards partnering with game developers to create games that promote their destinations. However, it also raises some important ethical questions. Is it right to use video games to promote tourism to sensitive or over-touristed areas? And what about the potential for video games to create unrealistic expectations of a destination? These are questions that the tourism industry will need to grapple with as virtual tourism becomes more mainstream. Ultimately, the rise of virtual tourism is a testament to the power of storytelling and the human desire for exploration. It is a reminder that, whether we are exploring the real world or a virtual one, we are all on a journey of discovery.

### Conclusion

In conclusion, the psychology of virtual tourism is a rich and fascinating field of study, one that is likely to become increasingly important in the years to come. It is a field that challenges our traditional notions of travel, of place, and of experience. It is a field that is full of humor, of irony, and of profound insights into the human condition. And it is a field that is, above all, a lot of fun. So, the next time you find yourself spending a long weekend exploring the virtual world of your favorite video game, don't feel guilty. You're not just playing a game; you're engaging in a new and exciting form of tourism. You're a digital pioneer, an intrepid explorer of the virtual frontier. And who knows, you might even be inspired to book a real-world trip. Just be sure to pack your sense of humor. You're going to need it.
references:
[1] Nikolić, N. (2025). Video game induced tourism - a critical literature review. Bizinfo Blace, 16(1), 1-10.

[2] Xie, S. (2025). A Study on the Mechanism of Video Games in Stimulating Tourism Intention Based on the CAC Framework. Economics & Management Review, 6(2), 31-42.

[3] Luong, V. H., & Manthiou, A. (2026). Video game-inspired tourism: A synergistic framework. Tourism Management, 112, 105282.
